## Plymouth Batch 13

## 2024/2025 Academic Year

## Final Report Structure

The report documents your project's products and the processes that supported their development. It will normally conform to the following structure:

- Coursework front cover page (provided in the DLE)
- Title with your cover page
- Table of Contents
- List of Figures and Tables (if applicable)
- The main body of the report
  - Chapter 01
    - Introduction
    - Existing systems and problem definition
    - Project aims and objectives
    - Scope of the project
  - Chapter 02
    - Requirement gathering techniques
    - Functional and non- Functional requirements
    - Features of application
  - Chapter 03
    - Use case diagram
    - High-level diagram
    - ER diagram (if applicable)
    - User interface of the developed System (include only a few important interfaces)
  - Chapter 04
    - Development methodology
    - Technologies and tools used
    - Future Implementation
  - Chapter 05
    - Individual contribution (a small paragraph for each member)
    - Github commit history along with Github repository link.
    - Project Source Code Link (Upload your Soure code to Plymouth OneDrive. Please note that you should provide access to the link for all users (evaluators), or you will get zero marks. Get the OneDrive link and copy and paste it into the Final Report). Failing to include the source code link, you will receive a mark of ZERO for your project.
- Reference List

Report: Style

- The report should be professionally presented and word processed with (approx.) 1.15 line spacing. Pages should be numbered consecutively except for the title page.
- Sections and sub-sections should employ nested numbering, e.g., Section 1, sub-sections 1.1, 1.2, etc., sub-sub-sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, etc. This is important in helping the reader to follow the structure of your report.
- You should adhere to accepted norms regarding referencing, paraphrasing, and plagiarism.
- Use good grammar, and make sure that you understand the proper uses of punctuation symbols such as: comma, semi-colon, colon, and apostrophe. Avoid overly long sentences: 30 words is generally too long.
- Avoid unnecessary repetition.
- The standard academic style (to which you should conform) is past tense, third-person.
- Use passive forms when possible (e.g., 'the software was tested' not 'I tested the software').
- Try to be formal in style; avoid slang and colloquial language.
- Avoid being conversational in style. Avoid personal pronouns such as 'I', 'me', 'my', 'we', 'you', 'he', 'she' and try to avoid other personalization's such as 'the author', and 'the student'. Do use phrases such as 'this project', 'the program', 'the respondents' (and do use 'it' or 'they' to avoid excessive repetitions of these). Refer to yourself ('the author' - possibly followed by 'I' or 'my') only when you are clearly stating your own personal opinion.

**Final Deliverables**

&nbsp;

**Final report**

- **The report max word limit _3_ 000 words**. Your reference list, bibliography, and appendices are not included in the word count. We suggest that your report should be no less than 1000 words.

# You should also upload an electronic (PDF) copy of your report to the DLE. Please upload a single PDF file

# Rename the PDF file with your Group number as follows

# Ex :- Group_7_Final_Report.pdf
